E1.reasoning: Initial assessment: Tariff policies create recession risk, but NBER typically declares recessions with long lag. Elevated but not majority probability.

E2.reasoning: Initial assessment: Tariffs are inflationary but 3.5% is a high threshold. CPI more likely to land in 2.5-3.5% range. Fed would respond hawkishly.

E3.reasoning: Initial assessment: Fed cut three times in late 2024 but tariff-driven inflation complicates the path. Two cuts plausible but faces headwinds.

E4.reasoning: Initial assessment: Crude faces downward pressure from trade wars and increased US production. Slight lean toward lower end-of-year prices.

E5.reasoning: Initial assessment: True coin-flip. S&P started near ATH. Tariff uncertainty vs AI earnings growth and historical 70% base rate for positive years.

E6.reasoning: Initial assessment: Unemployment around 4.1-4.2% in late 2024. Reaching 4.5% requires meaningful deterioration. Labor market has been remarkably resilient.

E7.reasoning: Initial assessment: Rates ended 2024 around 6.7-6.9%. More likely to hover in 6.0-7.0% range. Slowing economy and potential Fed cuts create downward pressure.

E8.reasoning: Initial assessment: Bitcoin near $100K in late 2024. Crypto-friendly regulation supports, but extreme volatility makes year-end level genuinely uncertain.

P2.reasoning: Initial assessment: 6-3 conservative SCOTUS with cases on agency deference, immigration enforcement, and removal power. Trump admin actively pushing boundaries.

P3.reasoning: Initial assessment: Federal cases dropped post-election. Georgia case faces procedural delays. A final judgment in 2026 is unlikely given procedural posture.

P4.reasoning: Initial assessment: Criminal convictions take years to develop. A full conviction within 2026 is a high bar. Historical base rate is low.

P5.reasoning: RESOLVED: Cabinet/senior WH official resigned or was fired in 2026.

P6.reasoning: Initial assessment: Requires bipartisan cooperation (unlikely) or reconciliation (limited). Slim House majority makes partisan legislation difficult. Decades of legislative failure on immigration.

P7.reasoning: Initial assessment: President's party almost always loses House seats in midterms. Slim GOP majority (~220-215). Historical average loss is 25+ seats.

P8.reasoning: Initial assessment: Republicans won strong Senate majority in 2024 (~53-47). 2026 map is more balanced. Flipping enough seats is structurally difficult.
